The saltwater crocodile is the largest
living reptile on Earth and inhabits a
vast and varied range across coastal
regions and river systems throughout
Southeast Asia, northern Australia, and
the Western Pacific. Its habitat spans
brackish estuaries, mangrove swamps,
tidal rivers, freshwater wetlands, and
even open seas. Crocodiles can even
climb trees. Unfortunately, this also
means they're quite good at climbing
fences. The average size of a saltwater
crocodile is around 17 ft or 5.2 m long
and 1,000 lb or 450 kg. But the largest
living specimen ever measured was an
individual nicknamed Lolong, who was
suspected of attacking several people
and killing two in the Philippines. He
was captured alive, though it took 3
weeks and 100 people to do so. He
weighed approximately 2,370
lb or 1,075 kg. He measured 20.24 24 ft
or 6.17 m in total length, making him
the largest known crocodile ever
reliably measured. Crocodiles in general
kill around 1,000 people per year. And
most of these are fatalities from
saltwater crocs or Nile crocs. The
number could even be higher, maybe even
as high as 4,500, because many deaths
from rural areas go unreported. For
reference, sharks kill around five or
six people per year. And it's not like
it is with some other animals who are
territorial or who mistake humans for
something else. Crocs very much eat
people on purpose because they're
hungry. They're one of the only
remaining true predators of humans. That
being said, dogs and even bee stings
easily kill more people than crocodiles
per year. And we don't walk through our
neighborhoods in terror. The fear we
feel of crocodiles is probably due to
their unfamiliar, gargantuan reptilian
form. My whole life, I've looked at them
and wondered, "Is standing next to them
like standing next to a dinosaur? Are
they basically dinosaurs that didn't
die?" While crocodiles are not
dinosaurs, they are relatives. Both are
part of a larger group called the
arosaurs, which also includes birds and
extinct terasaurs. Dinosaurs and
crocodiles share a common ancestor that
lived over 240 million years ago. That
ancestry split into two evolutionary
branches. One led to terasaurs,
dinosaurs, and eventually birds. The
other led to crocodilians. So,
interestingly, crocodiles are more
closely related to birds than to
lizards. So, while they are not
dinosaurs, their relatives lived
alongside them for millions of years.
And those crocodile ancestors survived
the mass extinction that killed most of
the dinosaurs 66 million years ago. And
they did so likely because they lived in
less affected habitats like underwater.
They needed less food to survive because
of their slow metabolism and ate
opportunistically. They also protected
their eggs in buried nests. And for
these reasons, our modern Earth is
graced with these incredible beasts. In
the past, there were all sorts of shapes
and sizes of crocodilians. Some
long-legged or even some bipeedal and
terrestrial. But the 26 species of
crocodilians that exist today are
lizardshaped reptiles that live at the
interface between water and land. This
video will focus largely on the
saltwater crocodile, but sometimes I
will show other species to illustrate
certain behaviors. Saltwater crocodiles
are well adapted to aquatic life with
webbed hind feet and eyes, ears, and
nostrils positioned at the top of their
head. Their nostrils and ears can be
partially sealed to keep water out. And
a third eyelid called the nictitating
membrane protects their eye while
swimming. And their entire body is
protected by skutes, which are thick
keratinized scales. In some spots, like
the back and the head, these are
reinforced with osteoderms, aka bony
plates. These bony plates give the
crocodile a sort of external skeleton
and can even deflect small caliber
rounds like from handguns or 22 caliber
rifles. The skuts along their tail and
back are also elongated and triangular
which helps increase surface area and
thus improve swimming maneuverability.
This protective layer is of course
important for protection against
predator attacks when they're young and
from other crocs when fighting over
territory. But it serves another
purpose. It allows the crocodiles to be
almost completely impervious to water
loss through their skin. Skin like ours
has some water resistance, but it's not
completely waterproof. We're constantly
losing water via sweat since our skin is
optimized for temperature regulation,
not water conservation. But for
saltwater crocodiles, it's another story
entirely. Their keratinized skoots are
designed to retain moisture in harsh
sunexposed environments, preventing
dehydration. And it also protects them
in another part of their habitat, the
salty ocean. Without watertight skin,
the salt water could draw water out of
the skin, causing dehydration and
damaging tissues. The ability to live
indefinitely in saltwater is remarkable
for a reptile that we think of normally
as a freshwater creature. Other reptiles
like sea turtles have evolved to live in
salt water, but they live their entire
lives in the sea. So, they're adapted
perfectly for salt. But to go between
both requires evolutionary
specialization that is almost unheard
of, and it requires more than just thick
skin. In fresh water, a crocodile must
deal with water constantly entering its
body through osmosis. In salt water, the
challenge is the opposite. Water tends
to leave the body. And yet, their blood
salt levels remain constant, as proven
by a 1975 study that measured crocodiles
blood salt levels across a salinity
gradient. They found no matter where the
crocodile was pulled from across the
gradient, their plasma osmotic
concentration stayed at 304 millles,
which is close to the 30% concentration
of seawater. It was clear that the
crocodiles were regulating this very
tightly. For many years, scientists
could not figure out how they did it.
The crocodiles didn't have salt glands
around their eyes like ocean birds or
turtles who basically cry the salt out.
or they didn't have rectal salt glands
like in sharks. But in 1980, they
checked somewhere that no one had
before, the mouth. It turns out the salt
gland is on the crocodile's tongue.
These arrows point to the salt gland
pores. It probably escaped notice for so
long because everyone is reluctant to
poke around in there. So these salt
glands in their tongue help them excrete
a concentrated solution of sodium and
chloride ions which then get expelled
through the nostrils. And because of
this incredible adaptability in their
range, saltwater crocodiles can eat a
huge variety of prey across different
ecosystems. They'll eat almost any
animal that enters their territory like
fish, crabs, turtles, birds, kangaroos,
walabeees, bats, buffalo, and even other
predators like bull sharks. Saltwater
crocodiles have been seen hundreds of
kilometers from shore. But before a
crocodile catches its prey, it must
first detect it. Their eyes, positioned
at the top of their head, scan the
environment for motion, while the rest
of the body remains almost completely
hidden beneath the surface.
Anatomically, their eyes are not very
different from our own, except the pupil
is a vertical slit. And while not fully
forward- facing like a predator, such as
a cat or an owl, crocodile eyes do have
slightly forward orientation, which
gives them some binocular vision.
Underwater, you might expect a predator
like this to have excellent vision, but
they don't. Plus, sometimes the murky
dark water would render even the best
eyes useless. And yet, saltwater
crocodiles have been observed to hunt
underwater in pitch darkness. This is
because they don't just see their
surroundings, they feel them. They scan
the environment with their entire body
using a network of specialized skin
sensors called integumentaryary sense
organs, also known as dermal pressure
receptors. These are among the most
sensitive and specialized tactile organs
found in any reptile. ISOs appear as
tiny raised dark dots on the skin,
typically measuring about half a
millimeter to 1 millimeter in width.
They're especially prominent on the
snout. Each of these little dots is one.
They can also be found across almost the
entire body, including the flanks, legs,
and tail at a rate of about one per
scale. Internally, each ISO contains a
cluster of nerve endings connected to
mechano receptors that respond to touch
and pressure. So, when a crocodile sits
motionless in the water, it will feel
any vibration, any ripple, or any water
disturbance caused by prey. and helping
snatch their prey are their massive
teeth. These teeth are conicle, non-
serrated, and curved slightly backwards,
which allows them to pierce and hold on
to slippery prey like fish or struggling
mammals. They are made of dentin covered
with enamel and have cementum at the
base. Their teeth frequently get broken
when capturing prey. So, crocodiles are
polyiodons, meaning that they replace
their teeth continuously throughout
their lives. A single individual may go
through 3,000 to 5,000 teeth over a
lifetime. When its prey comes within
range, the crocodile launches forward
with astonishing speed, using its tail
and limbs to lunge. It can run and swim
up to 18 mph or 29 km per hour, which is
three times faster than the fastest
human swimmer. This is kind of hard to
see, but it's a saltwater crocodile
swimming alongside a boat, sort of like
a dolphin. It can even be seen lunging
out of the water vertically, sometimes
as much as nearly its entire body
length. This is largely powered by their
insanely strong tail muscles. It then
snaps its jaws shut with a bite force
exceeding 3,700 lb per square in, the
strongest ever recorded of any living
animal in a controlled setting. Great
whites and orcas are probably more, but
no one's ever directly measured these.
There's an anecdotal report of a Nile
crocodile reaching 5,000 PSI, but this
was on a TV show and not a peer-reviewed
study. Much of the speed and power of a
croc's snap is powered by a scissoring
action performed by two muscle groups
set more or less at a right angle to
each other. Both the pseudo temporales
muscle and the huge dorsal tergoid
muscle originate deep within the skull.
And the coordination between these
muscles from opposite angles creates not
only speed but an almost unstoppable
closing force making escape nearly
impossible for any prey caught in its
jaws. But conversely, they are
relatively easy to keep closed. Duct
tape is usually enough. And without that
little feature, we probably would know
next to nothing about these creatures.
Once the prey is in its jaws, especially
if it's too large to swallow whole or

initiates the death roll. This is a baby

crocodiles. It shows that this behavior

always come back. One study showed
exactly the extent of this. Researchers,
including Steve Irwin, by the way,
transllocated three large male saltwater
crocodiles in northern Australia via
helicopter between 56 and 126 km from
their original capture sites. Satellite
telemetry was used to monitor their
locations after release. Croc A spent 13
days near its release site, then
traveled 99 km back over the course of
15 days. Croc B lingered for about 2
weeks at his new spot and then swam 57
km back to his original spot over the
course of 8 days. And Croc C did
something truly crazy. He had been flown
overland due east 126 km away from his
original spot across the Cape York
Peninsula. He lingered in his new spot
for 3 months. Then suddenly over a
20-day period, he traveled 411 km
swimming up and around the peninsula to
get back to his original spot. This
means he swam 20 km per day. Each
crocodile returned to the exact spot
where it had been captured. Are they
using local landmarks to do this?
Magnetic orientation like sea turtles
do? Sun and celestial cues or oldactory
cues? It's probably a mix of all of
these, but we don't know for sure. And
overall, the study showed that
relocating problem saltwater crocodiles
far away probably isn't the best idea.
Today, problem crocodiles are moved to a
farm or a sanctuary or killed for their
skin and meat. And it's not just this
incredible homing ability that makes
crocodiles so incredible. Crocodiles
also possess a rich and complex system

and continuing into adulthood. All

fertilization, lay eggs, and show

other reptiles, and shows a sensitivity
I never knew they were capable of.
Crocodile communication begins even
before hatching. Embryionic crocodiles
vocalize from within the egg to signal

coordinated emergence and summoning
their mother, who often digs them out
and gently carries them to the water in

predator on the planet. Hatchlings

level of parental care among reptiles.
And then once the crocodiles hatch,

communication in animals generally
refers to the use of signals that go

there's a few things scientists look for
when deciding if communication is
complex or not. If communication changes
based on context, like using different
signals for mating, courtship,
aggression, or parenting, that's a point
towards complexity. Another sign of
complexity is audience awareness, where
an animal adjusts its signals depending
on who's nearby. Even more sophisticated
is the presence of learning or cultural
transmission. And at the highest level
of complexity, some species use
referential signals, which are signals
that refer to specific things in the
environment, almost like words, like
when monkey species have specific alarm
calls for different predators.

When saltwater crocodile eggs were

Normally the way a crocodile hears sound

Coordinated hunting is exceedingly rare
in vertebrates. It's only known in a few
species of primates like chimps, a few
species of pack animals like wolves, a
few types of hawks and corvids, and in
things like dolphins and orcas. For a

only published observation of such a

He described cooperative fishing in

local on the island of Papua. A larger

the shore with splashes of its tail
while smaller crocodiles waited in the
shallows ready to snatch the cornered
fish. And while not solidified in the

and dozens of anecdotal reports of many

documented 17 instances of cooperative

alligator species. While rare, two cases
clearly showed collaboration, involving

giant saltwater crocodile hurting a pig
into shallow water where smaller crocs
lay in weight, suggesting that
